posts.text:
Resilience...

That was the word I chose for myself back in January as my compass for navigating this year with strength, care, and purpose. I never imagined how deeply it would resonate today.

This week has been incredibly difficult. Many talented, passionate, and kind colleagues were impacted by layoffs. While I was somehow fortunate to remain, I carry a heavy heart for those who didn’t. I want you to know that I see you. I admire your courage. And I believe in your next chapter.

Resilience isn’t about pretending things are okay. It is about honoring the hard moments, learning from them and still choosing to move forward. If you need a connection, a conversation, or just someone to listen, I’m here.

To everyone navigating uncertainty right now: you are not alone. You are valued. And you are resilient.

#Resilience #SupportEachOther #MentalHealthMatters

posts.text:
Estatisticas mundias estimam que "aprox. 70% das pessoas irão vivenciar sindrome de impostor pelo menos uma vez na vida" (https://lnkd.in/gP9p3fhn). Essa experiência afeta principalmente as mulheres e, caso não identificada e persistente, pode ter impacto considerável em nossa carreira. 
A comunidade Brasil do The WIT Network faz um convite a você para participar de um bate papo online sobre o tema. Venha conhecer mais sobre a Sindrome de Impostor, bem como ouvir as estorias e dicas de profissionais talentosos que vivenciaram e superaram essa experiencia. 
Data: 16 de Outubro, às 19h BRT. 

[English]
World statistics estimate that "around 70% of people in the world will experience Imposter Syndrome at least once in their lives"(https://lnkd.in/gP9p3fhn). It is likely to affect women. 
Non identified cases or persistently experiencing Imposter Syndrome may affect your career progression. With this in mind, the Brazilian Community of The WIT Network invites you to join an online roundtable about the subject. Join us to know more about Imposter Syndrome and hear the amazing stories from talented professionals that overcame it.
Date: October 16th at 7PM BRT (GMT-3) 
Note: the event will be held in Portuguese.

posts.text:
Yesterday I had the honor and pleasure of being a panelist in a University of Washington (UW) event promoted by SOMOS.tech 👩🏽‍💻 (We are tech) and Information School at UW.
We discussed about professional journey, and shared our interview insights and tips with the students who attended the event. 
 
I was very impressed and happy to see the engagement and enthusiasm of the students. They asked thoughtful and relevant questions and showed a genuine interest in learning more about how to pursue a career in this exciting and fast-growing area. 

I was also delighted to connect with my fellow panelists, Audrey Long, specialist in security @ Microsoft, and Victoria Wang, Product Manager @ Microsoft.  who are amazing professionals and role models in their respective areas of expertise.

I enjoyed sharing some interview tips that I learned in my career. These tips can help anyone who is looking for their ideal job. Here are some of them:

- Be yourself and honest during interviews. Focus on your strengths and eagerness to learn and grow.

- Do more than classes and college assignments to stand out. Show your passion and curiosity, collaborate with your colleagues in your own and their projects.
 
- Don't be scared of joining hackathons if you don't know the techs involved. Join them to learn and connect with people. You don't need to be an expert, you just need to be open-minded and willing to learn.

- Be proud and confident of your identity and culture, whether it's LATINX or another. Diversity is a strength, not a weakness. Share your stories and experiences that make you who you are and what you value.

- Experiment with and create your own projects using AI technologies. This will help you learn faster and keep up to date. There are many tools and platforms that you can use to try AI. Your College probably have some Azure credits for students (as Audrey mentioned well). Solve real-world problems that matter to you, or just have fun with AI and see what you can make. 

- Ask ChatGPT or Copilot to review your resume or help you prepare for an interview.

- In an interview, show concrete examples of how you collaborate with your peers at College or at your internship. Talk about one or two examples, pick your best collaboration case. Highlight the outcomes of your collaboration, and how you helped the group learn and succeed.

- To avoid sounding arrogant, when talking about your achievements and what you did, start with what your group did together and then move to the points where you made a difference and helped the group learn and succeed. This will show your humility, initiative and teamwork skills.

Thank you Luz A Mendoza García, Maho Pacheco, Lindsay Sullivan and everyone involved in the organization of the event.

resharedPost.text: We’re releasing a guide for teachers using ChatGPT in their classroom — including suggested prompts, an explanation of how ChatGPT works and its limitations, the efficacy of AI detectors, and bias. https://lnkd.in/gjHYKfSX

comments.text:
The single most important guide teachers can use is simply to teach and encourage their students to ask questions — to get curious and stay that way. Same for parents and their children.

Many students will love the fact that they can ask without any fear of condemnation, or being cautious of embarrassing the teacher by hitting the limits of what they know.

The first and most instinctive mechanism of learning for many animal youngsters is by following an example, usually by mimicking their parents. The one major ability that sets humans apart in this regard is that at some point, we can start asking questions (i.e. language), which is exactly what kids do as soon as they start speaking. It's in everyone, just sadly often silenced by parents or some teachers later in life.

The invention of the alphabet, the printing press, the telegraph, the radio, TV, the Internet — all accelerated the spread of information, which was amazing. But this one is a true weapon of mass education because it puts a pretty good teacher that's an expert in almost anything right at everybody's fingertips.

All the students have to do is ask questions. That's it. That's the key.

resharedPost.text:
One of the difficult and necessary conversations we need to have right now is with our children. But where do we begin?

Listen to A Bit of Optimism: How to Have Difficult Conversations, with David Harris at bit.ly/abitofoptimism

comments.text:
"The ability to form and recognize alliances is essential to social animals, and the ability to categorize individuals as friends or foes, or as inside or outside one's group, is a crucial cognitive predicate for alliances. In this view, bias and prejudice in humans are evolved forms of this otherwise useful capacity."

This is a quote from BluePrint, from Nicholas A. Christakis. It is an eye opener, as it helps to understand where such behavior comes from and how primitive it is. It also helps to understand that it is teachable, and therefore it shall work in both ways: we can teach love, instead of hate. 

To make another reference, Daniel Dennett wrote that "... my central policy recommendation is that we gently, firmly educate the people of the world, so that they can make truly informed choices about their lives. Ignorance is nothing shameful; imposing ignorance is shameful."  

While he wrote in the context of religion it shall work effectively for everything else. Let's have such difficult conversations, let's learn together, and transform this planet for the better.

posts.text:
The way you communicate with your team is an important component to the team's success.
In the process of communicating better, there is one step called feedback that sometimes is understood as giving compliments about a good work done. 
Feedback is broader and more complex. An important perspective on giving feedback that is for many times avoided by people is the negative (or constructive) feedback. 
In a 2018's article, HBR brought to our attention a research in which it was identified that people tend to avoid colleagues who give them more negative feedbacks then the view they have on themselves. According to same research, in these cases, people look for other groups to reinforce the better view. 
Although agreeing on that, I still believe that giving negative feedback is an important mechanism in the communication of leaders and teams, as long as it gives an honest, constructive perception about what needs to be improved and the value that the person represents to the company.
So, when giving perspectives on someone's work, apply these steps:
1. Focus on the fact without judgements or subjective opinions. 
2. Show the impact on someone else or on the job itself.
3. Give suggestion on how to improve and talk about the value that the person represents to the team/company.

comments.text:
Cheers, Marcia! Pretty inspiring.
I do believe in the force  - yes, the one from the stars, too!... ;-) of the focus on facts, this is the way. It takes us to systems and processes, not people.
You know... I see that the hard part is the culture, the organizational culture - how to prepare it to this feedback friendly environment.
I have been using the idea behind learning processes - the respect to the people's learning processes. Each one of us have different processes and 'bias'. What is obvious for myself is not for others. Sometimes common sense is not so common.
Relying on this maybe a personal PDCA/PDSA process should be used to provide feedback.

comments.text:
Misconceptions about Culture abound in Organisations where it is directed from the top as a 'behavioral contract':

1. Culture, in reality, becomes a 'bottom up' process once the leaders develop, initiate, and act as Role Models.

2. Culture is a dynamic living entity that needs fresh oxygen and fresh blood stream flowing through the entire organisation - through all employees regardless of their position or role in it. This can be achieved only through effective (*) teamwork throughout the organization, and by ensuring that every individual plays an active role in at least one team, and all teams are thoroughly trained in numerous hard and soft skills to help them - 
(a) enhance customer experience;
(b) maintain team harmony and guard against "group think";
(c) feel 'safe' to try, fail, learn, and improve individually and collectively.

3. Enlightened leaders acknowledge this to be their primary role, and this fact is reflected in their 'best of clas' performance for the benefit of ALL stakeholders.
(*) effective teams are goal-directed, inspired, engaged, empowered, and self-directed. They guard against violation of 'codes of conduct', that cover the entire gamut of interpersonal, cultural and healthy conflict management skills.

resharedPost.text:
Patrocinado pela IBM Brasil e Flex, em colaboração com a Apple, e executado pelo ELDORADO, o HACKATRUCK iniciou sua jornada pelo Brasil em outubro de 2015.
O HACKATRUCK foi um ambicioso projeto na área de educação. O laboratório itinerante tem por objetivo capacitar estudantes de instituições de Ensino Superior de Tecnologia da Informação em programação SWIFT para plataforma iOS.
Por três anos, o HackaTruck visitou 29 instituições de ensino superior brasileiras e capacitou 1.500 alunos em seu laboratório móvel, em temas como programação Swift para Plataforma iOS e ofertou ciclos de palestras técnicas, além de mais de 4.800 estudantes por meio de seus módulos online. Agora, encontra-se em sua nova fase: HackaTruck MakerSpace.
Numa verdadeira capacitação sobre rodas, o HackaTruck MakerSpace segue inspirado pelo conceito maker com seu laboratório móvel constituído em um makerspace, um espaço em que os alunos criam e desenvolvem protótipos de aplicativos relacionados aos temas estudados.
#ELDORADO20Anos #InspiraçãoPeloNovo #20AnosELDORADO #ELDORADO20 #historiadoELDORADO #pordentrodoELDORADO #institutoELDORADO

No dia 04/04, acontece o “LoRaWAN no Brasil – Presente e Futuro”, no ELDORADO. 
A tecnologia LoRaWAN, amplamente difundida e implementada em redes por diversos países, chega ao Brasil como uma opção de complementariedade de tecnologias, potencializando o mercado de Internet das Coisas (IoT), permitindo comunicação de longa distância e com baixo consumo de energia para as mais diversas verticais de negócios.
O evento contará com o apoio do Embarcados e do Instituto NCB, além da presença da American Tower – provedor de rede neutra LoRaWAN no modelo de atacado – e fabricantes de soluções, baseadas na tecnologia.
Será uma grande oportunidade para quem busca conhecer os planos de implantação da rede no Brasil e opções de hardware para projetos de dispositivos.
